title: International financial support to infrastructure
subtitle: Total official international support ([official development assistance](#dod:oda) plus other official flows) to infrastructure. This data is expressed in US dollars. It is adjusted for inflation but does not account for differences in living costs between countries.
note: This data is expressed in constant 2022 US$.
caption:
International financial support to infrastructure

Total official international support (official development assistance plus other official flows) to infrastructure. This data is expressed in US dollars. It is adjusted for inflation but does not account for differences in living costs between countries.

This choropleth world map displays 2023 levels of total official international financial support to infrastructure, expressed in constant 2022 US dollars. The legend ranges from $0 to $3 billion, with specific thresholds marked at $100 million, $300 million, and $1 billion. The darkest green band, representing values above $1 billion and extending toward $3 billion, includes India, China, Indonesia, and the Philippines. Brazil, Mexico, and South Africa sit in the next darkest band, falling between $300 million and $1 billion. A broad set of countries across Latin America, Southeast Asia, and parts of Africa—including Colombia, Peru, Vietnam, Kenya, and Nigeria—appear in mid-range green shades that correspond to the $100 million to $300 million range. Lighter green shades, indicating support below $100 million, cover much of Eastern Europe, Central Asia, and scattered nations in Africa and the Americas. North America, Western Europe, Australia, and Japan are shown with diagonal hatching, indicating no data for these regions. Greenland and Antarctica also appear hatched. The visual pattern shows the highest levels of support concentrated in large emerging economies in Asia and Latin America, while lower levels are distributed across smaller or less centrally positioned nations. No country appears in the absolute lowest band near $0; even the lightest shaded countries fall above that threshold. The map excludes high-income donor regions from direct comparison due to missing data.
